name: Nanna (Moon God)
shortDescription: Nanna, also known as Suen or Sin, was the Mesopotamian god of the moon. He was a central figure in Sumerian religion, revered as a source of light and order in the night sky.
description: Nanna, primarily known as the Sumerian moon god, held a prominent position in the Mesopotamian pantheon. He was the son of Enlil, the chief god of the Sumerian pantheon, and Ninlil. His primary center of worship was Ur, where the Great Ziggurat of Ur was dedicated to him. As the moon god, Nanna symbolized wisdom, guidance, and the cyclical nature of time. He was believed to control the tides and was associated with cattle breeding, reflecting the lunar cycle's influence on agriculture and animal husbandry. The crescent moon was his primary symbol. Myths depict him traversing the heavens in a boat, illuminating the night. The Akkadians later adopted him as Sin. Nanna's importance extended to divination, as lunar phases were interpreted for omens. His wife was Ningal, and their children included Inanna (Ishtar), the goddess of love and war, and Utu (Shamash), the sun god. He represented divine authority and cosmic order, playing a vital role in the Sumerian worldview and their understanding of the universe.

sources.title: The Electronic Text Corpus of Sumerian Literature
sources.author: University of Oxford
